title: Advancing Health Equity Research
text:
My name is Seungju Kim1 and thank you for visiting my personal website  detailing my research interests and work!
My research is broadly focused advancing the science of LGBTQ+ health disparities by looking at  socio-ecological sources of stigma and resilience.

title: Advancing Health Equity Research

title: Creating APA-7 Ready Correlation Tables
text:
One of the most common, and yet painstaking, tasks in writing a manuscript is creating a table (correlation, regression, etc.).
Creating them by hand is not only time-consuming but also error-prone. Even when some softwares (SPSS) allow users to create tables, they are often not APA7 ready.
Today, I introduce a simple package called {apaTables} that will prepare an APA7 ready correlation table (the most commonly reported APA table) in 3 steps AND less than 5 minutes!
Note
This assumes that you have basic familiarity with R.

title: Creating APA-7 Ready Correlation Tables
text:
Modify Output.
The full arguments are below.

apa.cor.table(
  trees,
  filename = NA, # (optional) a filename must end in .doc or .rtf
  table.number = 0, # optional, you can add in Word.
  show.conf.interval = TRUE, # Optional, and most would make this FALSE.
  show.sig.stars = TRUE,
  show.pvalue = TRUE, # unfortunately, it will only show ** < .01, which is why you should keep this TRUE to add a third *** for < .001.
  landscape = TRUE
)







Important



Unfortunately, it will only show * <.05 or ** < .01, which is why you should keep show.pvalue = TRUE to manually add a third *** for < .001.

title: Moderation Regression and Simple Slopes in Lavaan
text:
Typically, when we run a linear regression in R, we will use the lm() function. And while the lm() will get most jobs done, it has several limitations:

It cannot handle missing data.
It does not have robust standard errors estimators built in.

This is in contrast to Mplus which has both of these features, with capabilities for a host of estimators (e.g., maximum likelihood with robust standard errors) and the ability to handle missing data using full information maximum likelihood estimator.
Well the great news is that you too can do this in R using the lavaan package. Finally, often times I hear that people have a hard time plotting simple slopes using lavaan, but I will be going over a very helpful function to help us!
Let’s begin!

title: Moderation Regression and Simple Slopes in Lavaan
text:
Import Data
For our example today, we will be using the ACTIVE (Advanced Cognitive Training for Independent and Vital Elderly) dataset available here

# data <- read.csv(here("resource_page", "active.csv"))

Great we’ve imported our dataset! Let’s take a look at the dataset!

title: Moderation Regression and Simple Slopes in Lavaan
text:
Introduce Missingness

# na_data <- data %>%
#   mutate(across(everything(), ~{
#     # For each value, 15% chance of becoming NA
#     ifelse(runif(n()) < 0.15, NA, .)
#   }))

Great! Let’s take a look at the data now.

title: Moderation Regression and Simple Slopes in Lavaan
text:
Examine Missingness

# pacman::p_load(naniar) # install/load the "naniar" package
# 
# miss_var_summary(na_data)

We can see here that about 15% of all data is missing. Let’s now run our regression model!

title: Blitz Presentation for SCRA 2025
text:
Note



This presentation was given at the 2025 Biennial Society for Community Research and Action.



Abstract
In this study, we sought to understand how Sexual and Gender Minorities of Color engage with their sexual/gender identity and religiosity/spirituality and how different patterns of engagement related to social and mental health. We identified four patterns of engagement, suggesting higher religious engagement is tied to lower mental distress but higher internalized stigma. In summary, we find religious engagement is tied to both health benefits and risks.


Presentation
To view the presentation file, please see below.




Open Materials
To view all reproducible materials (e.g., code, data), please see the github repository below.

title: Seungju Kim
text:
My name is Seungju Kim1 and thank you for visiting my personal website detailing my research interests and work!
I am a mental health researcher, National Science Foundation Graduate Research Fellow, and  third-year PhD student in Clinical/Community Psychology Program at the University of Illinois.

title: Seungju Kim
text:
Research Interests
My research is ultimately informed by my commitment to creating lasting change for the mental and behavioral health of LGBTQ+ people through three lines of research:

I use longitudinal methods to map the dynamic pathways through which stigma, resilience, and social context accumulate over time to produce mental health disparities among LGBTQ+ individuals.
I invest in the meta-scientific infrastructure of LGBTQ+ health research — through systematic review, meta-analysis, and creating software — to strengthen how the field measures, synthesizes, and communicates evidence.
I examine how religion and spirituality across socioecological levels — from individual beliefs to structural policy — shape mental health for LGBTQ+ individuals, with particular attention to conservative religious contexts.

title: Seungju Kim
text:
Education


2024 B.A. in Psychology Wheaton College Magna cum laude


2026 M.S. in Psychology University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign


2031 (expected) Ph.D. in Clinical/Community Psychology University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign

title: Seungju Kim
text:
Training
My research is grounded in a deep commitment to the flourishing of LGBTQ+ people. My training spans the full spectrum of religious and clinical contexts — from conservative evangelical institutions to affirming community settings — reflecting my conviction that achieving health equity for LGBTQ+ people, particularly youth in conservative families and churches, requires building bridges across differences rather than around it.
Prior to beginning my doctoral studies, I earned my B.A. in Psychology magna cum laude from Wheaton College, where I completed an honors thesis examining psychological well-being in religious mixed-orientation marriages during my junior year. My research training began as a freshman working with Dr. Sandra Rueger, before joining Dr. Mark Yarhouse at the Sexuality and Gender Identity Institute, where I contributed to a national study investigating the complex intersection of faith and LGBTQ+ identities.
Expanding my research training before senior year, I established significant external collaborations with leading researchers. At Utah State University, I worked with Dr. Tyler Lefevor examining the effects of religiousness and spirituality on LGBTQ+ mental health, where I developed skills in psychometric scale development and validation using R’s {psych} and {lavaan} packages.
Simultaneously, I joined Dr. David Wang at Fuller Theological Seminary, analyzing longitudinal data from a $1.9 million study on spiritual formation. This collaboration strengthened my experience in sophisticated longitudinal modeling using R and Mplus, examining complex patterns of change over time.
My quantitative methodological expertise was further enhanced when I was selected for the competitive NSF-REU Health Equity summer internship at the University of Nebraska-Lincoln. Working with Drs. Katie Edwards and Lorey Wheeler on multiple NIH-funded studies examining LGBTQ+ youth and emerging adults, I gained advanced skills for identifying mischievous responders in survey data, regression models, and scale development. I was later recruited as a post-bac research associate at the UNL methodology and psychometrics center.
Before entering graduate school, I was awarded the National Science Foundation Graduate Research Fellowship, among one of the only awardees at Wheaton.
